title: Animals That Change Their Body Heat
summary: Some animals can change their body temperature to survive tough times. Most mammals and birds keep a steady temperature, called homeothermy. But others, like the fat-tailed dwarf lemur, can change their temperature a lot in one day. This ability is called heterothermy. Scientists are learning that many animals use heterothermy to stay safe from dangers like storms, floods, and predators. For example, bats use torpor, a deep sleep, when it's cold or rainy to save energy. Sugar gliders also use torpor during big storms to stay warm in their nests. This helps them live through bad weather and find food later. Researchers use tiny devices to study these animals in the wild. By understanding heterothermy, we learn how animals adapt to their environments.

animals that fly and use torpor in bad weather
small animals that use torpor in storms

What do bats use torpor for?

questions.question: How do sugar gliders use torpor?
options: During big storms to stay warm
questions.correct_answer: During big storms to stay warm

background_read: Animals have different ways to survive. Some, like humans, keep their body temperature the same all the time. Others can change it to handle challenges like cold weather or lack of food. This article talks about animals that use this ability, called heterothermy, to outlast dangers. Scientists use tools to watch these animals in nature and understand how they live through tough times.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists like Danielle Levesque and Fritz Geiser study animals.
WHAT: Animals use heterothermy to change body temperature and survive dangers.
WHERE: In places like Australia and labs where animals live.
WHY: To learn how animals adapt to harsh environments and stay safe.

perspectives.description: Scientists research how animals use temperature changes to survive.
